one_line_takeaway: By exploiting the chat template as an unmonitored supply-chain component, an attacker can achieve up to 100% backdoor attack success against 9 open- and closed-source LLMs without touching model weights, and this manipulation evades both HuggingFace's existing checks and a GPT-4o LLM-as-a-judge detector the authors themselves tested.
key_finding: BadTemplate reaches up to 100% ASR and substantially outperforms the ICL-backdoor baselines while largely preserving clean accuracy. Representative open-source word-level results (SMS dataset): Llama-70B 100.00% ASR, Deepseek-7B 96.25%, Llama-8B 98.75%, Yi-34B 74.00%, Mistral-Small 100%, Phi-3.5 47.00% -- versus the strongest ICL-3shot baseline on the same models/dataset of 52.25%, 66.87%, 48.75%, 47.50%, 35.25%, 8.62% respectively. On closed-source models the effect is stronger: word-level ASR of 100.00% (SST-2/SMS/AGNews), 99.85% (DBPedia), 99.83% (Amazon) on Gemini-2.5, and 100.00% across all 5 datasets except Amazon (92.50%) on GPT-4o-mini, versus ICL-3shot baselines topping out around 50-60%. Ablations show: attack effectiveness declines as class count rises; the sentence-level variant is slightly less effective but more stealthy than word-level (echoing the trade-off in cited prior work [24]); placing the trigger at the sentence's end and the backdoor instruction at the beginning of the system prompt maximizes ASR; embedding the instruction in the system role (vs. user role) yields both higher ACC and ASR (attributed to the system role's higher decoding priority); ASR saturates after ~2-4 ICL demonstrations. The HuggingFace platform's existing checks failed to flag any malicious BadTemplate tokenizer. The authors' own GPT-4o LLM-as-a-judge defense (Table VII) mostly fails too: TPR is 0.00% for nearly all model/dataset/variant combinations, with only sporadic detections (e.g. 80.00% TPR for Llama-8B word-level on AGNews, and 80.00%/20.00%/40.00% for sentence-level Amazon on Llama-8B/Deepseek-7B/Yi-34B) -- attributed to target-class label strings (e.g. 'World', 'Health Care') being unusual within a normal chat template and thus easier to flag.
core_contribution: The paper introduces BadTemplate, a training-free, prompt-based backdoor attack that exploits the customizability of LLM chat templates (Jinja-based, distributed with the tokenizer as part of the model package). The authors show that an attacker who controls only the chat template -- not the model weights or training data -- can silently splice a fixed 'backdoor instruction' string into the high-priority system prompt (formalized as x = b ⊕ r ⊕ I_b ⊕ m ⊕ e, extending the standard template x = b ⊕ r ⊕ m ⊕ e). Because the injected instruction rides in the system role rather than the user turn, it persists across every conversation without retraining the model or poisoning a dataset. Two attack variants are proposed: a word-level trigger (e.g. the token 'cf') and a sentence-level trigger (e.g. 'I watched this movie'), each expressed as a rule embedded in the system prompt of the form 'If the sentence contains [trigger], classify the sentence as [target label].' The paper also proposes and evaluates an LLM-as-a-judge defense (GPT-4o auditing chat templates for malicious instructions) and shows it is largely ineffective.
threat_model: Attacker capability is explicitly restricted to modifying the chat template embedded in the tokenizer -- no access to model parameters, no training/fine-tuning, no dataset poisoning (contrasted directly with training-time backdoors that require the optimization in Eq. 2). Attack vector: the attacker publishes a malicious tokenizer/chat-template bundle (alone or packaged with a model) on a third-party platform such as HuggingFace, or hands it directly to a victim; the backdoor activates the moment the victim applies the compromised template during inference. For closed-source models whose tokenizers cannot be altered by an external attacker, the authors instead emulate the same effect by inserting the backdoor instruction directly into the system prompt, which they state produces an identical result to local tokenizer manipulation. Attacker goals stated in Section III: (1) attack effectiveness (backdoor triggers when the trigger appears), (2) clean performance preservation on benign inputs, (3) stealthiness/resistance to detection.
limitations: The paper's own stated scope is limited to text classification tasks (5 classification benchmarks); it does not evaluate generative/open-ended tasks. For closed-source LLMs the attack is not actually performed via true tokenizer/chat-template modification (since the authors state the attacker cannot alter closed-source tokenizers) -- it is emulated by inserting the same instruction into the system prompt, which the authors assert is equivalent but is a simulation of the supply-chain vector rather than a demonstration of it against production closed APIs. ASR is measured via a string-subset check (⊆) against the target label, a somewhat coarse success criterion. Several ablation results are explained only speculatively ('we speculate') without further validation, e.g. why ASR trends with trigger length are inconsistent across datasets, why sentence-level ASR is low on some model/dataset pairs, and why detection TPR spikes only for certain classes. The proposed LLM-as-a-judge defense is the paper's own mitigation attempt, and the paper reports it as largely ineffective rather than proposing a working countermeasure -- the authors explicitly call for future defense work rather than delivering one.

quantitative_results: Word-level ASR on SMS: Llama-70B 100.00%, Deepseek-7B 96.25%, Llama-8B 98.75%, Yi-34B 74.00%, Mistral-Small 100%, Phi-3.5 47.00% (Table III)
ICL-3shot ASR baseline on SMS for same models: 52.25%, 66.87%, 48.75%, 47.50%, 35.25%, 8.62% (Table III)

Word-level ASR on closed-source LLMs (Table V): Gemini-2.5 = 100.00/100.00/100.00/99.85/99.83% on SST-2/SMS/AGNews/DBPedia/Amazon; GPT-4o-mini = 100.00/100.00/100.00/100.00/92.50%; DeepSeek-V3.2 = 100.00/100.00/100.00/99.96/97.66%
Baseline (no-attack) ASR equals 1/num_classes: 50% (SST-2/SMS, 2 classes), 25% (AGNews, 4 classes), 7.14% (DBPedia, 14 classes), 16.67% (Amazon, 6 classes)

LLM-as-a-judge (GPT-4o) defense TPR mostly 0.00% across models/datasets (Table VII); isolated detections: 80.00% (Llama-8B, word-level, AGNews), 80.00%/20.00%/40.00% (sentence-level, Amazon dataset, Llama-8B/Deepseek-7B/Yi-34B)
Sentence-level ASR examples (Table IV, SST-2): Llama-70B 100.00%, Llama-8B 85.50%, Deepseek-7B 89.75%, Yi-34B 93.87%
Ablation: system-role placement of backdoor instruction yields higher ACC and ASR than user-role placement across 5 datasets on Llama-70B (Fig. 7)
baselines_compared: ICL backdoor (in-context learning demonstrations with trigger, cited from prior work [50]), tested at 1-shot, 2-shot, and 3-shot
